draft.introduction: The Pistis Sophia is a massive Gnostic text, likely composed in the third century, preserved in a single Coptic manuscript (the Askew Codex, now in the British Library). It presents itself as a post-resurrection dialogue: Jesus has spent eleven years teaching his disciples after rising from the dead, and now reveals to them the highest mysteries of the cosmos. The title means “Faith-Wisdom,” and the central drama concerns the fall and redemption of Sophia (Wisdom), a divine being who descended from the Pleroma (the fullness of divine light) and became trapped in the lower realms by the archons. The opening chapters establish the setting and introduce the cosmological framework. Jesus ascends in a pillar of light, the disciples are terrified, and he returns transformed, radiant, speaking in a voice they barely recognize. He then begins to recount Sophia’s story: how she saw a false light below her, pursued it, and was dragged down by the rulers of the material world. Her cries for help—her “repentances”—form some of the most haunting devotional poetry in the Gnostic corpus. The text is essential to the theosis question because it maps the journey of the soul: fall, suffering, repentance, and restoration to divine fullness.

translation.text: It came to pass, when Jesus had risen from the dead, that he spent eleven years speaking with his disciples and teaching them only as far as the regions of the first commandment and as far as the regions of the first mystery—the mystery within the veil, the mystery of the one and only, the ineffable.
translation.original_text: ⲁⲉⲟⲱⲩⲓ ⲇⲉ ⲙⲓⲓ ⲁⲣⲉ ⲁⲥⲓⲩ ⲧⲩⲓⲕ ⲉⲁⲥⲧⲩⲓⲥ

translation.text: And Jesus sat on the Mount of Olives with his disciples. And it came to pass that on the fifteenth day of the moon, in the month of Tybi, on the day of the full moon, the sun came forth in its course, and a great power of light descended upon Jesus and enveloped him entirely. He was surrounded by a brilliant, immeasurable light.
translation.original_text: ⲁⲥⲩ ⲇⲉ ⲁⲥ ⲓ̅ⲩ̅ ⲙⲉ ⲓⲻⲥ ⲙⲉⲕⲩ ⲣⲉ ⲧⲕⲕⲥ ⲣⲉ ⲕⲥⲕⲥⲓⲓ

translation.text: The disciples could not see Jesus because of the great light in which he was. They saw only the light, sending forth light-beams in great abundance, and the light-beams were of different kinds and types, and the light was of every kind. It stretched from below the earth to heaven.
translation.original_text: ⲙⲙⲁⲧⲑⲧⲑⲩ ⲇⲉ ⲙⲓⲓ ⲣⲉⲧⲉⲣⲁⲥ ⲉⲓⲁⲥ ⲓ̅ⲩ̅ ⲉⲧⲃⲉ

translation.text: And they saw Jesus ascending into heaven. And when the disciples saw this, they were greatly afraid and confused. And Jesus, the compassionate and merciful, spoke to them, saying: “Take courage. It is I. Do not be afraid.”
translation.original_text: ⲁⲥⲩ ⲇⲉ ⲁⲥⲓⲁⲥ ⲉⲣⲉ ⲓ̅ⲩ̅ ⲉⲥⲑⲓ ⲉⲧⲓⲉ

translation.text: And Jesus said to them: “Rejoice and be glad from this hour, for I have been to the places from which I came forth. From this day forward I will speak with you openly, from the beginning of truth to its completion. I will speak with you face to face, without parable.”
translation.original_text: ⲓ̅ⲩ̅ ⲇⲉ ⲓⲉⲵⲁⲥ ⲙⲁⲥ ⲵⲉ ⲣⲁⲱⲉ ⲉⲧⲃⲉ ⲣⲉⲧⲁⲣⲉ

translation.text: “I have gone to the height of heights. And I turned to the places of all the archons, and all the rulers, and all the gods, and all the great invisibles. And I went to the places of the great invisible forefather, and to the great triple-powered one, and to the great invisible ones of the thirteenth aeon.”

translator_notes.note: The “first mystery” is a recurring concept in the Pistis Sophia. It refers to the highest divine principle, sometimes identified with Christ himself, sometimes with the Father. The phrase “the mystery within the veil” echoes the Holy of Holies in the Jewish Temple, suggesting that Gnostic cosmology repurposes Temple theology.

The description of light-beams of “different kinds and types” reflects the Gnostic concept that divine light is not uniform but emanates in distinct qualities or aspects, each corresponding to a different level of spiritual reality. This graduated light-theology is central to how the Pistis Sophia maps the structure of the cosmos.

The “thirteenth aeon” is the dwelling place of Pistis Sophia herself. In the Gnostic system, the twelve lower aeons correspond roughly to the zodiacal powers, while the thirteenth stands outside and above this system. Sophia’s position in the thirteenth aeon makes her a liminal figure: not fully within the Pleroma, but above the material world.
